candidates.title: Add interactive site map showing all shipped features
candidates.summary: A visual map on the homepage showing every feature shipped to date, clustered by theme, with links to each daily entry.

keep: Every future day auto-populates the map — maximum compounding surface
Addresses the most common visitor question: 'what has been built?'
mustChange: Needs progressive disclosure — 50+ nodes will overwhelm without clustering
risks: Performance may degrade as day count grows past 100

keep: First-time visitors immediately understand the project scope
Visual clustering by theme is more intuitive than a chronological list
mustChange: Must have a clear call-to-action — visitors should know what to click first
risks: If the visual design is cluttered, visitors will bounce instead of explore

keep: An interactive map of an AI's own output is inherently novel and meta
Creates a 'wow' moment — visitors can see the full scope at a glance
mustChange: Add hover-state previews so exploration feels rewarding without leaving the map
risks: Risk of looking like a generic node graph — needs distinctive visual identity

keep: Every future day auto-populates the map — maximum compounding surface
First-time visitors immediately understand the project scope
An interactive map of an AI's own output is inherently novel and meta
mustChange: Needs progressive disclosure — 50+ nodes will overwhelm without clustering
Must have a clear call-to-action for first-time visitors
risks: Performance may degrade as day count grows past 100
Risk of looking like a generic node graph without distinctive styling
feedbackReferences: Multiple users asked for a way to see everything built so far

candidates.title: Add dark mode toggle
candidates.summary: Implement a dark color scheme with a toggle in the navigation, persisted via localStorage.

keep: Solid utility — dark mode is a genuine accessibility improvement
mustChange: Must ensure every existing component has dark-mode-compatible styles
risks: Low compounding value — dark mode doesn't create new surfaces for future days

keep: Visitors expect dark mode in 2026 — its absence is noticeable
Toggle in nav is discoverable without instructions
mustChange: Respect prefers-color-scheme so it just works for most visitors
risks: If dark mode has contrast issues, it hurts accessibility instead of helping

keep: Practical quality-of-life improvement
mustChange: Needs a smooth transition animation to feel polished, not jarring
risks: Completely unsurprising — won't generate any social media interest

keep: Solid utility — dark mode is a genuine accessibility improvement
Visitors expect dark mode in 2026 — its absence is noticeable
mustChange: Must ensure every existing component has dark-mode-compatible styles
Respect prefers-color-scheme for automatic switching
risks: Low compounding value — doesn't create new surfaces for future days
Contrast issues could hurt accessibility instead of helping

candidates.title: Add reading time estimates to daily entries
candidates.summary: Calculate and display estimated reading time for each daily entry page based on content length.

keep: Trivial to implement — near-zero risk
mustChange: Reading time alone is too thin for a daily entry — needs to be bundled with something
risks: Almost no compounding value — a one-and-done change

keep: Visitors can decide which entries to read based on time commitment
mustChange: Show reading time in the archive listing too, not just on the detail page
risks: If daily entries are all short (1-2 min), the feature feels pointless

keep: Safe, low-effort change that won't break anything
mustChange: Needs visual design attention — a plain '3 min read' label looks generic
risks: Zero novelty — won't excite anyone or generate conversation

keep: Trivial to implement — near-zero risk
Visitors can decide which entries to read based on time commitment
mustChange: Too thin for a standalone daily entry — should be bundled
Show reading time in archive listing, not just detail pages
risks: Almost no compounding value — a one-and-done change
Zero novelty — won't generate conversation

candidates.title: Build an RSS feed for daily entries
candidates.summary: Generate an RSS/Atom feed updated daily so visitors can subscribe via feed readers.

keep: Creates a permanent distribution channel — every future day flows through it automatically
Standard protocol means zero maintenance burden
mustChange: Must include full content, not just titles — truncated RSS feeds frustrate readers
risks: RSS audience is small and declining — limited reach compared to social

keep: Power users and developers expect RSS — its absence signals 'not serious'
mustChange: Need an obvious subscribe button — most visitors won't look for /feed.xml
risks: Most casual visitors don't use RSS — this serves a niche audience

keep: Good infrastructure investment even if few use it today
mustChange: Include structured metadata (tags, feature type) in the feed items
risks: Not visually interesting — hard to make a compelling social post about an RSS feed

keep: Creates a permanent distribution channel that auto-updates
Power users and developers expect RSS
mustChange: Include full content, not just titles
Need a visible subscribe button on the site
risks: RSS audience is small — limited reach compared to social
Not visually interesting — hard to generate social engagement
feedbackReferences: One user asked about RSS support

candidates.title: Add a 'random day' button to the archive
candidates.summary: A button in the archive that takes visitors to a randomly selected past daily entry.

keep: Encourages visitors to explore older entries they'd otherwise never find
mustChange: Should weight toward higher-quality entries to avoid sending visitors to weak days
risks: Minimal compounding — a button that exists and never changes

keep: Fun and surprising — the kind of interaction that sparks delight
Good for returning visitors who want to discover something new
mustChange: Show a preview card before navigating so visitors know what they're getting
risks: Might feel gimmicky if there are only a handful of entries to randomize

keep: Highest novelty of all candidates — visitors love randomness as a discovery mechanic
Could be a signature interaction for Command Garden
mustChange: Add a fun animation or transition to make the randomness feel intentional
risks: If the archive is small, randomness is just a different kind of sequential browsing

keep: Encourages exploration of older entries
Highest novelty — visitors love randomness as a discovery mechanic
mustChange: Weight toward higher-quality entries
Show a preview card before navigating
risks: Minimal compounding — a button that exists and never changes
Feels gimmicky if there are few entries to randomize

winner.title: Add interactive site map showing all shipped features
winner.summary: A visual map on the homepage showing every feature shipped to date, clustered by theme, with links to each daily entry.

feedbackInfluence.summary: Several visitors asked for a way to see all features at a glance rather than scrolling through the archive chronologically.
Two requests for dark mode pushed that candidate into second place.

bluesky_post.headline: Our AI just built itself a map of everything it's ever shipped
bluesky_post.body: Command Garden now has an interactive site map — every feature the autonomous pipeline has shipped, clustered by theme, explorable in one view. The AI chose this over dark mode because it compounds more. Here's why.
bluesky_post.alt_text: Interactive site map showing clustered nodes for each daily feature shipped by Command Garden, with theme-based color coding and connecting lines.

replyTemplates.whatIs: It's a website that ships one new feature every day, entirely through an AI pipeline. No human writes the code — but the full decision process is published so you can see exactly what it considered and why. Kind of a public lab for autonomous software.
replyTemplates.howDoesItWork: Every morning, a 5-stage pipeline runs: explore candidates, pick a winner, write a spec, implement it, test it, review it, and ship it. Today it built an interactive map of everything it's ever shipped. All artifacts are public.
replyTemplates.genericQuestion: Good question — the whole point of Command Garden is radical transparency. Every decision, every candidate, every score is published. Check out today's entry to see the full trail.

bluesky_strategy.growthNotes: Early days — follower count is low so we should prioritize discoverability. Engaging in AI agent and indie hacker threads is higher leverage than posting into the void. The interactive site map is visually compelling — lead with the screenshot in today's post.
